content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : File                                           Date : Nov. 24, 1976

                                                         96-11-120

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ken Finney

Subject: Telephone conversation with Mark Landsbomb.

       After talking to Gina Kutchins, Mr. Landsbomb called me
to discuss a potential conflict of interest involving the
Mayor of the City of Tribune (located in the San Gabriel
Valley). Mr. Landsbomb is a reporter for a newspaper in
the area.

       I cautioned Mr. Landsbomb at the outset that my remarks
were not for attribution and that, with respect to the
specific facts involving the Mayor, I was speaking off the
record. I indicated to him that this was because the
prescribed methods for determining a conflict of interest
are either for the public official to seek an opinion of
the Commission or for a potential complainant to file a
complaint with either the District Attorney or the FPPC.
Accordingly, I stated that it would not be appropriate
for him to attribute remarks to an official of the FPPC
which suggested that there is a conflict of interest.

       Speaking of the record, I first outlined to Mr. Landsbomb
the various applicable provisions of the Act and my under-
standing of how they related to the situation involving
the Mayor (my information about the situation involving
the Mayor was based on my discussion with Gina). The
relevant provisions were Section 87103(c), Section 87100
and Section 82030. In addition, the regulation on materiality, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, is relevant. I
cited each of these sections to Mr. Landsbomb and suggested
that he consult them.

       The Mayor's situation is as follows. He was not able to
obtain financing for the purchase of a new car but the
new car dealer nevertheless permitted him to use the car
free of charge for a period of approximately five months
(from August, 1974, to January, 1975). Either during or
some time after the Mayor used the car free of charge, he
apparently attempted to influence the City Council with
respect to the relocation of certain boundary lines of a

Memorandum to File
Page 2
NOV. 24, 1976

proposed redevelopment project so that the car dealer would
be included in the redeveloped zone. I did not ascertain
what the specific impact of being located in the redevelopment area was, but instead spoke to Mr. Landsbomb about what
would be required under the Political Reform Act.

With respect to the Act, I indicated to Mr. Landsbomb that
the free use of the car undoubtedly constituted a gift and
that the amount of the gift probably exceeded $250. Accordingly, the car dealer became a source of income to the Mayor.
I then indicated to Mr. Landsbomb that a public official cannot participate in any decision which will have a material
financial effect on a source of income when the income was
received by or promised to the official in the last 12 months.
As to materiality, I indicated to Mr. Landsbomb that it was
impossible to determine, without further facts, whether the
activities of the Mayor would involve a material financial
effect and I directed him to the regulation on materiality.

Mr. Landsbomb and I also discussed two other issues. The
first was how the 12-month period would be measured. In
other words, would it commence from the last month in which
the Mayor had the free use of the car or from the first
month? I stated to Mr. Landsbomb that this issue had not
been decided by the Commission and that I, therefore, could
not definitively answer his question. Also, we discussed
the effect of the dates, i.e., that the car had been used
during 1974 (prior to the effective date of the Political
Reform Act). I stated that I did not think this was particularly important since the Mayor's influencing activities apparently occurred after the effective date of the Act and
therefore were subject to its provisions. Moreover, those
provisions would be applicable to any source of income within the last 12 months, even if part of that 12-month period
occurred prior to the effective date of the Act.

Finally, Mr. Landsbomb and I briefly discussed the Commission's
jurisdiction and its potential means of enforcing the Act.
In this respect, I described to him the nature of an administrative enforcement proceeding and the Commission's authority
to go to court in a civil action. I also discussed with him
the fact that the Commission's jurisdiction with respect to
local officials is an open question at this time. Moreover,
I suggested to Mr. Landsbomb that if he wanted to file a complaint the appropriate authority to contact would be the District Attorney. He indicated to me that he had been in touch
with the District Attorney about a possible investigation.

KF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: This is an internal FPPC memorandum documenting a telephone conversation with a reporter regarding a potential conflict of interest involving the Mayor of Tribune, California, who allegedly received a free car from a dealer and then attempted to influence city council decisions benefiting that dealer.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal memorandum to file, not an official FPPC advice letter response to a formal request. It documents an informal off-the-record conversation with a reporter. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. The FPPC official (Finney) discussed applicable law (sections 87103(c), 87100, 82030, and regulation 18702) but explicitly stated remarks were not for attribution and the situation required formal complaint or official request procedures.

embedding.summary: Internal FPPC memorandum documenting Ken Finney's off-the-record telephone conversation with reporter Mark Landsbomb regarding a potential conflict of interest involving the Mayor of Tribune, where the Mayor received free use of a car from a dealer and subsequently attempted to influence city council regarding the dealer's inclusion in a redevelopment project.
